executive_brief: The cybersecurity landscape has reached a critical inflection point where artificial intelligence is collapsing the vulnerability lifecycle. Anthropic's Mythos AI model has demonstrated the capability to autonomously discover and exploit thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across major operating systems, compressing the window between discovery and weaponization from months to hours [4, 11, 254]. This shift is already visible in the wild: the Marimo Python notebook vulnerability (CVE-2026-39987) was exploited within 10 hours of disclosure, and Adobe Reader zero-days have been active for months without patches [144, 188]. Defenders can no longer rely on traditional patch cycles; resilience must now be architectural. Simultaneously, state-sponsored actors are transitioning from espionage to physical disruption. Iran-linked groups are actively targeting internet-exposed Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in US critical infrastructure, while a separate campaign compromised Venice's flood defense systems [8, 198]. The convergence of AI-accelerated exploitation and OT physical targeting means that a software flaw can now trigger real-world infrastructure failure with unprecedented speed.

threat_stories.headline: The Mythos Effect: AI Collapses the Zero-Day Window
threat_stories.narrative:
The era of the 'patch Tuesday' safety net is over. Anthropic's Project Glasswing and the Mythos AI model have publicly demonstrated the ability to identify and exploit zero-day vulnerabilities at a scale and speed that human analysts cannot match [26, 202]. This is not theoretical; the Marimo Python notebook platform suffered a critical pre-authentication RCE (CVE-2026-39987) that was weaponized in the wild within 10 hours of public disclosure [144, 177]. Attackers are leveraging AI to automate the fuzzing and exploit generation process, removing the friction that previously protected defenders. 

This acceleration is forcing a fundamental change in defense strategy. Organizations relying on reactive patching are now operating in a state of perpetual exposure. The Adobe Reader zero-day (CVE-2026-34621), exploited for months prior to patching, highlights the danger of waiting for vendor fixes [188, 216]. With AI agents like Claude Code also vulnerable to prompt injection attacks that bypass safety rules, the attack surface is expanding into the development lifecycle itself [93, 324]. Security leaders must shift from 'patch and pray' to 'assume breach and segment,' prioritizing micro-segmentation and runtime application self-protection (RASP) over perimeter controls.

timeline.event: Anthropic announces Mythos AI capabilities to find zero-days
timeline.significance: Signals AI-driven vulnerability discovery is now operational

timeline.event: Marimo CVE-2026-39987 disclosed and exploited within 10 hours
timeline.significance: Demonstrates new speed of exploitation post-disclosure

timeline.event: Adobe patches CVE-2026-34621 after months of exploitation
timeline.significance: Confirms long-term zero-day activity despite vendor awareness

timeline.event: Microsoft Patch Tuesday addresses 167 flaws including 2 zero-days
timeline.significance: Highlights volume of vulnerabilities outpacing patch cycles

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. All organizations using enterprise software, AI agents, or data science tools (Marimo, Flowise) are at immediate risk. The speed of exploitation renders standard patch SLAs ineffective.
threat_stories.action_required: 1. Isolate internet-facing Marimo instances immediately and upgrade to v0.23.0+. 2. Disable JavaScript in Adobe Reader until patched. 3. Implement network segmentation to prevent lateral movement from compromised endpoints. 4. Audit AI agent permissions (GitHub Actions, Claude Code) for prompt injection risks.

threat_stories.headline: Iranian Actors Target US Critical Infrastructure PLCs
threat_stories.narrative:
Iran-affiliated threat actors, including groups like CyberAv3ngers, have escalated from espionage to active disruption of Operational Technology (OT). Since November 2023, at least 75 devices have been compromised, but recent scans reveal over 5,000 internet-exposed Rockwell Automation PLCs globally, primarily in the US [8, 189]. Attackers are leveraging default credentials and unpatched legacy protocols (EtherNet/IP, Modbus) to gain remote access to industrial control systems. The goal is no longer just data theft; it is operational sabotage. 

This threat is not isolated to the US. In a parallel incident, hackers claimed control over Venice's San Marco anti-flood pumps, demonstrating the ability to manipulate physical infrastructure via exposed OT interfaces [198]. The attackers utilized leased overseas infrastructure and legitimate configuration tools like Studio 5000 Logix Designer to blend in with normal traffic [20]. This convergence of IT and OT vulnerabilities means a standard network breach can now lead to physical shutdowns of energy grids, water treatment, and manufacturing facilities.

timeline.event: FBI/CISA/NSA issue joint advisory on Iran-linked PLC attacks
timeline.significance: Official confirmation of active targeting of US critical infrastructure
Quantifies the massive attack surface available to adversaries

timeline.event: Hackers claim control of Venice flood defense pumps
timeline.significance: Proof of concept for physical disruption via OT compromise

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical for Energy, Water, and Manufacturing sectors. Direct risk to public safety and physical availability of essential services.
threat_stories.action_required: 1. Disconnect all PLCs from the public internet immediately. 2. Block ports 44818, 2222, 102, 22, and 502 from external IPs at the firewall. 3. Change default credentials on all OT devices. 4. Implement network segmentation to isolate OT networks from corporate IT.

threat_stories.headline: Supply Chain Poisoning: From CPUID to ChipSoft
threat_stories.narrative:
Trust in software supply chains is eroding rapidly. In a coordinated wave of attacks, the CPUID website was hijacked to distribute trojanized versions of CPU-Z and HWMonitor, delivering the STX RAT to unsuspecting users [166, 187]. Simultaneously, the Smart Slider 3 Pro plugin for WordPress and Joomla was poisoned via compromised Nextend servers, pushing backdoored updates to over 800,000 installations [118, 123]. These are not opportunistic hacks; they are strategic compromises of trusted update mechanisms. 

The impact extends to healthcare, where Dutch vendor ChipSoft was hit by ransomware, forcing hospitals in the Netherlands and Belgium to revert to manual patient records [21, 167]. This highlights the fragility of centralized service providers. When a single vendor is compromised, the entire ecosystem collapses. Attackers are targeting the 'seams' between organizations—third-party SDKs, update servers, and service providers—because they offer a higher return on investment than direct targeting.

timeline.event: CPUID website hijacked to serve STX RAT
timeline.significance: Compromise of trusted hardware utility downloads
Supply chain attack affecting 800k+ web installations

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Affects general public (CPUID), web developers (Smart Slider), and healthcare (ChipSoft). Indicates a shift towards supply chain compromise.
threat_stories.action_required: 1. Revert Smart Slider 3 Pro to version 3.5.1.34 or earlier. 2. Verify CPU-Z and HWMonitor downloads via checksums from official GitHub repositories. 3. Audit third-party SDKs (e.g., EngageLab) for sandbox bypass vulnerabilities. 4. Implement strict vendor risk assessments for critical service providers.

threat_stories.headline: Forest Blizzard Hijacks Routers for Global Espionage
threat_stories.narrative:
Russian state-sponsored group Forest Blizzard is executing a sophisticated campaign targeting home and small-office routers to enable Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) attacks. By compromising devices like TP-Link and MikroTik, attackers redirect DNS traffic to intercept credentials and session cookies, specifically targeting Microsoft Outlook sessions [22, 24, 32]. This bypasses traditional email security controls because the interception happens at the network layer before the data reaches the user's device. 

The campaign leverages legitimate tools like `dnsmasq` to spoof DNS responses, presenting invalid TLS certificates that users often accept without scrutiny [24]. The US government has disrupted parts of this operation, but the infrastructure remains resilient [40]. This technique is particularly dangerous for remote workers, as it turns their home network into a surveillance node. It proves that endpoint security is insufficient if the underlying network infrastructure is compromised.

timeline.event: Forest Blizzard router hijacking campaign identified
timeline.significance: Confirms active targeting of SOHO network infrastructure
Government intervention confirms national security threat level

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High for remote workers and organizations with BYOD policies. Compromised routers allow credential theft and session hijacking.
threat_stories.action_required: 1. Enforce DNS over HTTPS (DoH) on all corporate devices. 2. Disable split tunneling on corporate VPNs. 3. Require hardware tokens (FIDO2) for authentication to bypass session cookie theft. 4. Update firmware on all home routers used for business.

threat_stories.headline: Ransomware Evolution: Storm-1175 and Medusa
threat_stories.narrative:
Ransomware groups are adapting to the AI landscape by deploying malware within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure. Storm-1175 has been observed deploying Medusa ransomware against healthcare and education sectors in the UK, US, and Australia immediately after security flaws are disclosed [1]. This 'rapid response' exploitation model suggests attackers are using automated scanning and deployment tools to capitalize on the window of exposure before patches are applied. 

The targeting of healthcare remains a priority, as seen with the ChipSoft attack, but the speed of deployment is the new differentiator. Attackers are no longer waiting for a 'Patch Tuesday' cycle; they are hunting for unpatched systems in real-time. This requires defenders to move from scheduled patching to continuous vulnerability management and automated response capabilities.

timeline.event: Storm-1175 deploys Medusa within 24 hours of flaw disclosure
timeline.significance: Demonstrates automated exploitation of newly disclosed vulnerabilities

timeline.event: ChipSoft ransomware attack disrupts Dutch hospitals
timeline.significance: Continued focus on healthcare sector availability

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical for Healthcare and Education. Immediate risk of service disruption and data loss.
threat_stories.action_required: 1. Implement automated vulnerability scanning that triggers immediate isolation of exposed assets. 2. Ensure offline backups are immutable and tested regularly. 3. Restrict administrative privileges to reduce ransomware lateral movement.

actor_profiles.name: Storm-1175
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of vulnerability disclosure. Targeting healthcare and education sectors.

actor_profiles.name: CyberAv3ngers
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Targeting internet-exposed Rockwell/Allen-Bradley PLCs to disrupt critical infrastructure. Claiming control of Venice flood pumps.

actor_profiles.name: Forest Blizzard
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Hijacking SOHO routers to conduct AiTM attacks against Outlook sessions. Targeting TP-Link and MikroTik devices.

actor_profiles.name: APT28
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying PRISMEX malware via RTF file exploitation targeting Ukraine and NATO allies. Using WebDAV connections for payload delivery.

actor_profiles.name: Lazarus Group
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Spreading malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go, Rust. Using Facebook social engineering to deliver RokRAT. Supply chain attacks on Axios.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Zero-day vulnerability allowing arbitrary code execution via malicious PDF JavaScript. Actively exploited for months prior to patch.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Install emergency updates released by Adobe immediately. Disable JavaScript in Reader settings.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Pre-authentication RCE via terminal WebSocket endpoint. Exploited within 10 hours of disclosure.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to Marimo version 0.23.0 or later. Isolate instances from network.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution. Added to CISA KEV catalog.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply latest Ivanti security patches immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical flaw exploited for remote code execution in AI workflow management.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Flowise to latest version addressing CVE-2025-59528.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Unrestricted file upload vulnerability allowing RCE on unpatched servers.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update to version 2.8.7 or later immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Spoofing vulnerability exploited in the wild prior to Patch Tuesday.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply April 2026 Patch Tuesday updates immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Privilege escalation vulnerability dubbed BlueHammer. Public exploit code available.
Command injection flaws in Perforce VCS driver enabling arbitrary command execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Composer to latest patched version.

hunting_leads.title: Marimo WebSocket Exploitation
hunting_leads.context: Attackers are exploiting the unauthenticated /terminal/ws endpoint in Marimo to gain shell access.
hunting_leads.query: CommonSchema:NetworkFlow | where DestinationPort == 8787 | where DestinationUrl contains "/terminal/ws" | where UserAgent contains "curl" or "python"

hunting_leads.title: Adobe Reader Zero-Day Traffic
hunting_leads.context: Malicious PDFs exfiltrate data via HTTP/HTTPS using 'Adobe Synchronizer' strings.
hunting_leads.query: CommonSchema:NetworkFlow | where ProcessName == "AcroRd32.exe" | where DestinationUrl contains "Adobe Synchronizer" | where BytesSent > 1000

hunting_leads.title: PLC Port Scanning Activity
hunting_leads.context: Iran-linked actors scan for exposed EtherNet/IP (44818) and Modbus (502) ports.
hunting_leads.query: CommonSchema:NetworkFlow | where DestinationPort in (44818, 502, 2222, 102) | where SourceLocation == "External" | where DestinationLocation == "OT"

hunting_leads.title: Router DNS Hijacking
hunting_leads.context: Forest Blizzard compromises routers to redirect DNS traffic for AiTM attacks.
hunting_leads.query: CommonSchema:DNSQuery | where QueryName == "*.outlook.com" | where ResponseAddress != "KnownOutlookIPs" | where SourceDevice == "Router"

hunting_leads.title: AI Agent Credential Theft
hunting_leads.context: AI agents integrated with GitHub Actions are being hijacked to steal API keys via prompt injection.
hunting_leads.query: CommonSchema:ProcessCreate | where ParentImage contains "github-actions" | where CommandLine contains "curl" or "wget" | where CommandLine contains "token"

statistics.key_changes: The time-to-exploit for critical vulnerabilities has dropped from 61 days to under 10 hours due to AI automation. State actors are shifting from data theft to physical infrastructure disruption.
emerging_trends: Supply chain poisoning of update mechanisms
